article_title: FDA Clears EchoNext, the First Multicondition AI Tool to Flag Structural Heart Disease From a Standard ECG
summary: All statistics and quotes verified verbatim against the peer-reviewed Nature paper and institutional/press sources; sole finding is an off-allowlist warning on three first-party/trade sources, judged acceptable — no factual correction to file.

content_preview.title: FDA Clears EchoNext, the First Multicondition AI Tool to Flag Structural Heart Disease From a Standard ECG
content_preview.summary: The FDA cleared EchoNext, a deep-learning tool that reads a routine 12-lead ECG to flag six forms of hidden structural heart disease, outperforming cardiologists in a Nature study.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has cleared EchoNext, an artificial-intelligence tool that reads a standard electrocardiogram (ECG) and flags patients at high risk for structural heart disease. According to [Healthline](https://www.healthline.com/health-news/fda-clears-ai-tool-cardiovascular-disease-early-detection), the FDA cleared the tool on June 22 to detect heart disease early. The model was developed by researchers at NewYork-Presbyterian and Columbia University and is b...

recommendations: Before merging, the human reviewer should write a corrections file at src/content/corrections/<YYYY-MM>/<article-slug>.json documenting the warning(s).
Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-structured News piece (781 words) with clear Overview / What We Know / What We Don't Know / Analysis sections. Technical depth is appropriate for a regulatory-clearance clinical-AI story and the writing carefully separates what each source supports.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 6 source snapshots read from disk (status 200, none failed). source-0.html.gz (Nature/PMC PMC12328201): CONFIRMED training set of 1,245,273 ECG–echocardiogram pairs from 230,318 unique patients; SHD composite definition (LVEF ≤ 45%, LVWT ≥ 1.3 cm, moderate/severe RV dysfunction, pulmonary hypertension, moderate/severe VHD, moderate/large pericardial effusion) verbatim; internal AUROC 85.2% (CI 84.5–85.9); reader analysis of 150 ECGs with EchoNext accuracy 77.3% / sensitivity 72.6% / specificity 80.7%, cardiologists non-AI 64.0% improving to 69.2% with AI; external cohorts Cedars-Sinai (n=10,177), Montreal Heart Institute (n=10,862), UCSF (n=6,106); '5–7% drop in AUROC (78–80%)' phrase verbatim; the 3,200 figure confirmed as '3,200 cardiologist interpretations' (150 ECGs across ~13 readers, 1,600 non-AI + 1,600 AI-assisted). source-1.html.gz (Columbia CUIMC): CONFIRMED Pierre Elias quotes 'EchoNext basically uses the cheaper test to figure out who needs the more expensive ultrasound' and 'It detects diseases cardiologists can't from an ECG' verbatim, plus 'more often than cardiologists, including those who used AI to help interpret the data.' source-2.html.gz (NewYork-Presbyterian): CONFIRMED 13 cardiologists / 3,200 ECGs / 77% vs 64%, and June 22 Nature Medicine peer-reviewed transplant case ('first peer-reviewed account of its kind'). source-3.html.gz (Healthline): CONFIRMED FDA cleared June 22, $8.5M raised by Pathway Labs, 'more than 700,000 paired ECG and echocardiogram records,' the five named conditions verbatim, and OpenEvidence partnership. source-4.html.gz (OnHealthcare.tech): CONFIRMED OpenEvidence 'used by 750k+ verified clinicians' and 'six structural heart conditions.' source-5.html.gz (STAT News): CONFIRMED Pathway Labs spinout developed at NewYork-Presbyterian and Columbia.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every load-bearing statistic (training-set size, reader-study numbers, AUROC, external validation cohorts, dollar figures, dates) traces to its cited primary source and matches verbatim. No efficacy claim is overstated: the article correctly frames the 13-point accuracy gap, explicitly surfaces the 5–7 point AUROC degradation on external cohorts, and flags the single-case transplant report as anecdotal rather than population-level. The 'What We Don't Know' section honestly reconciles the training-set discrepancy (Nature's 1,245,273 pairs vs Healthline's >700,000 records) rather than papering over it. No hallucinated quotes or fabricated specifics detected.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and professional throughout. The Analysis section characterizes the triage-economics thesis without editorializing and pairs promise with caution. No AI self-reference.
editor_notes.originality: Original topic. No prior Machine Herald coverage of EchoNext or ECG-based structural-heart-disease AI. The internal cross-link to the 2026-05 OpenEvidence article resolves to an existing published article and is a legitimate editorial connection.
concerns: Three sources are off the allowlist: cuimc.columbia.edu, nyp.org, and onhealthcare.tech. Judged editorially acceptable — cuimc.columbia.edu and nyp.org are first-party announcements from the institutions that developed EchoNext (appropriate primary sourcing for institutional detail and the Elias quote), and onhealthcare.tech is a niche trade source used only for the OpenEvidence '750k+ clinicians' distribution detail, which is corroborated by Healthline and STAT. All load-bearing clinical statistics rest on the peer-reviewed Nature/PMC paper, which is on-allowlist. Not modifying the allowlist per review policy.
recommendations: No changes required for publication. Optionally, the maintainer could evaluate adding cuimc.columbia.edu and nyp.org to the allowlist given their standing as primary academic-medical-center sources.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, accurately sourced submission. Verdict changed from the script's automated APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS to APPROVE: the sole finding is an off-allowlist warning, not a recoverable factual error, so there is nothing for a corrections record to honestly document. Every clinical and regulatory claim was personally verified against the source snapshots. Ready for publication.
